title: The heart & blood vessels — your body's pump
examTip: The heart is extremely popular with AQA examiners. You need to name the four chambers, four valves, and the vessels entering and leaving each chamber — and explain why the left ventricle wall is thicker.
introTips: This lesson explains exactly how that pump is built — four chambers, four valves, and the vessels that carry blood in and out.
AQA often asks you to trace the full path of blood through the heart, so we'll make sure you can do that step by step.
You'll also compare the three types of blood vessel and explain why each one is built differently — structure always follows function.
intro: Every minute, your heart pumps about five litres of blood around your body — that's your entire blood supply, circulating once per minute, every minute of your life. It does this without stopping, without taking a break, for 70-odd years. This lesson is about how it actually works.

keyPoints.heading: Double circulation — why you need it
keyPoints.content: Humans have double circulation: blood passes through the heart twice for every full trip round the body. First, the right side pumps blood to the lungs (to pick up oxygen). Then the left side pumps that oxygen-rich blood to the rest of the body. Two separate circuits, one after the other. Fish only have single circulation — that's why they're slower.
writeBullets: Double circulation = blood passes through the heart twice per full circuit
Pulmonary circuit: right side of heart → lungs → left side of heart
Systemic circuit: left side of heart → body → right side of heart
This keeps oxygenated and deoxygenated blood separate

keyTerms.def: A circulatory system where blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circuit of the body

keyPoints.memTip: Double = through the heart TWICE. Right side → lungs (pulmonary circuit). Left side → body (systemic circuit). Sketch a figure-8: heart in the middle, lungs on one loop, body on the other. That diagram is the whole concept.

keyPoints.heading: The four chambers
keyPoints.content: The heart has four chambers. The top two are atria (singular: atrium) — they receive blood coming in. The bottom two are ventricles — they pump blood out. Right side = deoxygenated blood heading to the lungs. Left side = oxygenated blood heading to the body. The left ventricle has a much thicker muscular wall because it has to push blood all the way round the body.
writeBullets: Atria (×2): receive blood entering the heart
Ventricles (×2): pump blood out of the heart
Right side: deoxygenated blood → lungs
Left side: oxygenated blood → body
Left ventricle wall is THICKER — it pumps to the whole body (higher pressure needed)

keyTerms.def: Upper chamber of the heart; receives blood from veins
Lower chamber of the heart; pumps blood into arteries

keyPoints.memTip: Atria receive, Ventricles pump — A for Arrive, V for Vacate. The left ventricle thicker question is almost certain to come up: the answer is always 'it pumps to the whole body, needs higher pressure'. Say it out loud until it's automatic.

keyPoints.heading: The valves — keeping blood flowing one way
keyPoints.content: Valves stop blood flowing backwards. There are four valves. The atrioventricular (AV) valves sit between each atrium and ventricle: the tricuspid on the right, the bicuspid (mitral) on the left. The semilunar valves sit at the base of the aorta and pulmonary artery, stopping blood falling back into the ventricles after each beat. When valves don't close properly, blood leaks back — that's a heart murmur.
writeBullets: Tricuspid valve: between right atrium and right ventricle
Bicuspid (mitral) valve: between left atrium and left ventricle
Semilunar valves: at the base of the aorta and pulmonary artery
All valves prevent backflow of blood
Faulty valves → heart murmur (blood leaks back)

keyTerms.def: Valve between an atrium and ventricle; prevents backflow when the ventricle contracts
Valve at the base of the aorta or pulmonary artery; prevents backflow into the ventricles

keyPoints.memTip: Two pairs: AV valves (between atrium and ventricle) — tricuspid on the right, bicuspid on the left. Semilunar valves at the exits (aorta and pulmonary artery). Tricuspid = 3 flaps, bicuspid = 2 flaps — the numbers match.

keyPoints.heading: The major vessels
keyPoints.content: Four major blood vessels connect to the heart. The vena cava brings deoxygenated blood in to the right atrium. The pulmonary artery takes it out to the lungs. The pulmonary vein brings oxygenated blood back to the left atrium. The aorta takes it out to the body. The coronary arteries branch off the aorta and supply the heart muscle itself — block these and you have a heart attack.
writeBullets: Vena cava → right atrium (deoxygenated blood from body)
Pulmonary artery → lungs (deoxygenated blood, but it's still called an artery)
Pulmonary vein → left atrium (oxygenated blood from lungs)
Aorta → body (oxygenated blood — largest artery)
Coronary arteries → heart muscle itself (block = heart attack)

keyTerms.def: Large vein returning deoxygenated blood from the body to the right atrium
The largest artery; carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle to the body
Artery that supplies the heart muscle with oxygen and glucose

keyPoints.memTip: Learn the route as a story: dirty blood arrives via Vena cava → right side → Pulmonary artery to lungs → gets cleaned → Pulmonary vein back → left side → Aorta out to body. Say that story from memory. The pulmonary artery is the one that trips people up — it's an artery carrying deoxygenated blood.

keyPoints.heading: The three types of blood vessel
keyPoints.content: Blood travels through three types of vessel. Arteries carry blood away from the heart under high pressure — they have thick, elastic, muscular walls and a narrow lumen. Veins carry blood back to the heart under low pressure — they have thinner walls, a wider lumen, and valves to stop backflow. Capillaries are the tiny exchange vessels — one cell thick, so substances like oxygen and glucose can diffuse in and out easily.
writeBullets: Artery: thick muscular wall, narrow lumen, high pressure, no valves, away from heart
Vein: thin wall, wide lumen, low pressure, valves present, towards heart
Capillary: one cell thick wall, allows diffusion of O₂, CO₂, glucose, urea
Remember: A = Away (arteries go away from heart)

keyTerms.def: Thick-walled vessel carrying blood away from the heart at high pressure
Thin-walled vessel with valves, carrying blood to the heart at low pressure
Microscopic vessel with walls one cell thick; site of exchange between blood and tissues

keyPoints.memTip: A = Away (arteries go away from heart). Veins have Valves. Capillaries are one Cell thick. For the comparison table: arteries have thick walls + narrow lumen; veins have thin walls + wide lumen; capillaries are microscopic. Copy the table once from memory — that's worth more than reading it five times.

tables.title: Major vessels entering and leaving the heart

2.question: Why does the left ventricle have a thicker muscular wall than the right ventricle?
options: It pumps blood to the lungs, which are far away
It pumps blood to the whole body, so needs more force
2.correctFeedback: Exactly — it needs to generate more pressure to push blood all the way round the body, so it has more muscle.
2.wrongFeedback: The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood into the aorta and round the entire body — that takes a lot more force than the short trip to the lungs. More force means thicker muscle.

4.question: Which vessel carries oxygenated blood AWAY from the heart to the body?
4.correctFeedback: Right — the aorta is the largest artery and carries freshly oxygenated blood out of the left ventricle to the body.
4.wrongFeedback: The aorta leaves the left ventricle and carries oxygenated blood to the body. The vena cava brings deoxygenated blood in. The pulmonary artery goes to the lungs (deoxygenated). The pulmonary vein brings oxygenated blood back from the lungs.

commonMistakes: Saying the pulmonary artery carries oxygenated blood — it's an artery but it carries deoxygenated blood. 'Artery' means away from the heart, not oxygenated.
Forgetting that the left ventricle wall is thicker — this comes up often. Always explain WHY: it pumps to the whole body, needing higher pressure.
Mixing up atrium and ventricle — atria are the top receiving chambers, ventricles are the bottom pumping chambers.
Not naming specific valves — 'a valve' won't get the mark. Learn tricuspid (right AV), bicuspid/mitral (left AV), and semilunar.
examTips: AQA often asks you to 'trace the path of a red blood cell'. Practise writing: vena cava → right atrium → tricuspid valve → right ventricle → semilunar valve → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary vein → left atrium → bicuspid valve → left ventricle → semilunar valve → aorta → body.
When describing blood vessels in 6-mark questions, always compare all three (artery, vein, capillary) and link structure to function — e.g. 'capillaries are one cell thick to allow rapid diffusion'.
The word 'pulmonary' always means lungs. Pulmonary artery = to lungs. Pulmonary vein = from lungs.

revisionCardBullets: Double circulation: blood goes through the heart TWICE per circuit — once for lungs, once for body
Right side: receives deoxygenated blood from body (vena cava), sends it to lungs (pulmonary artery)
Left side: receives oxygenated blood from lungs (pulmonary vein), sends it to body (aorta)
Four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle
Left ventricle wall is THICKER — pumps to entire body so needs higher pressure
Valves: tricuspid (right AV), bicuspid/mitral (left AV), semilunar (at aorta and pulmonary artery)
Coronary arteries supply the heart muscle — blocked = heart attack
Artery: thick elastic wall, narrow lumen, high pressure, away from heart, no valves
Vein: thin wall, wide lumen, low pressure, towards heart, has valves
Capillary: one cell thick — for diffusion of O₂, CO₂, glucose, urea with tissues
